elements.sent-2411-261: In this section, we demonstrate how to use our matrix moment formulation to derive bounds on the EFT coefficients for several example EFTs.
elements.sent-2411-262: Most (but not all) of these positivity bounds for these models have been previously calculated using other methods.
elements.sent-2411-263: Therefore, our goal here is not to obtain comprehensive bounds for these models, but rather to illustrate how to compute the bounds using our approach, showcasing both consistency with and improvements over previous results.
elements.sent-2411-264: Let us first consider the case of a single scalar, in which case the matrix moments reduce to the normal moments and some necessary conditions of the moment problem have been used to bound general EFT coefficients via a pure bi-variate formulation \cite{Chiang:2021ziz (see \cite{Arkani-Hamed:2020blm,Bellazzini:2020cot for earlier work).}}
elements.sent-2411-265: Here we revisit this problem with our mixed-variate moment formulation, which is numerically more efficient.
elements.sent-2411-266: With the continuous large $\ell$ approximation, we implement the sufficient conditions for both the uni-variate and bi-variate parts of the moment problem.
elements.sent-2411-267: We find that these sufficient conditions generally give rise to small improvements to the previous results from the necessary conditions in the moment approach, and the bounds derived from these sufficient conditions agree to a high degree with the results obtained using the method of \cite{Caron-Huot:2020cmc .}

elements.section-2411-005: Inverse problem: from IR coefficients to UV spectrum
elements.sent-2411-347: As already mentioned, mathematically, a moment problem asks whether there exists a possible measure $\mu_{\mathbf{ij }$ for a given moment sequence $\{\mathfrak{m _{\mathbf{ij ^{\gamma_1\gamma_2 \mid (\gamma_1, \gamma_2) \in \Theta \}$ such that an integral representation \eqref{momentpro0 exists \cite{Schmdgen2017TheMP .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-348: If there exists a measure for such integrals, it is said that the moment problem is {\it solvable , and this measure is a representing measure.}
elements.sent-2411-349: In other words, the solvability condition discussed in Section \ref{sec:MP is the necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of a representing measure.}
elements.sent-2411-350: Physically, solving a specific moment problem is to determine whether there exists a UV spectrum for the given values of the EFT coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-351: Additionally, we may also ask whether it is possible to reconstruct a UV spectrum by the given set of EFT coefficients and whether the inferred UV spectrum is unique or the determinacy of the moment problem.
elements.sent-2411-352: In this section, we shall discuss how to reverse engineer the UV model from the IR information of the EFT.
elements.sent-2411-353: We will start with a toy (mathematical) example of truncated Hamburger moment problem.
elements.sent-2411-354: In Section \ref{sec:MassSpec , we connect the problem of reconstructing the UV mass spectrum from the forward amplitude to a Hausdorff moment problem and demonstrate the reconstruction with the the Veneziano amplitude.}
elements.sent-2411-355: In Section \ref{sec:AllSpec , we generalize the problem to the case of a non-forward amplitude, which allows us to reconstruct the UV spectrum of the masses and couplings from a given IR moment sequence.}
elements.sent-2411-356: In Section \ref{sec:EFT_gives_Moments , we demonstrate how to derive the moment sequence from the EFT coefficients in the amplitude.}
elements.sent-2411-357: In Section \ref{sec:Determinacy , we briefly discuss the mathematical concept of determinacy of a moment problem and its connections to uniqueness of the inferred UV spectrum.}
elements.sent-2411-358: In Section \ref{sec:SpecExamples , we illustrate the concepts and the methods in the UV reconstruction with explicit examples.}
elements.sent-2411-359: Let us first illustrate how to compute the representing measure or the UV spectrum with a toy example.
elements.sent-2411-360: Suppose there is a solvable $\Theta(3)$-truncated Hamburger (uni-variate, $1\times 1$ matrix) moment sequence $\set{\mathfrak{m ^0, \mathfrak{m ^1, \mathfrak{m ^2, \mathfrak{m ^3}$, which means they has the following integral representation with at least one possible measure $\mu$ \begin{equation}m^{\gamma} = \int_{R} x^\gamma \dif \mu, \gamma = 0,1,2,3.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-361: From Section \ref{sec:TruncatedMP , we know the following Hankel matrix shall be PSD ($\mathfrak{m}$ in $\mathcal{H _{1 ^{\mathfrak{m }$ will be omitted if there is no ambiguity): \begin{equation}H_{1} (2) \equiv  m^0 m^1 m^1 m^2 .\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-362: Besides, the Hankel matrix shall have an eventual flat PSD extension.
elements.sent-2411-363: For simplicity, we further assume that, given the first $4$ moments, there exists an $\mathfrak{m ^4$ such that the extended Hankel matrix \begin{equation}H_{1} (4) \equiv  m^0 m^1 m^2 m^1 m^2 m^3 m^2 m^3 m^4 \end{equation} satisfies $\mathcal{H _{1 (4) \succeq 0$ and $\mathcal{H _{1 (4)$ is flat, i.e., $\operatorname*{rank \mathcal{H _{1 (4) = \operatorname*{rank \mathcal{H _{1 (2)$.}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-364: Moreover, we also assume that $\operatorname*{rank \mathcal{H _{1 (2) = 2$, which implies $\mathfrak{m ^{0 >0, ~\mathfrak{m ^{2 > 0,~ \mathfrak{m ^{0 \mathfrak{m ^{2 - (\mathfrak{m ^{1 )^2 > 0$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-366: Given these assumptions, it is possible to prove this moment problem is really solvable by actually finding a possible set $\set{(T^n, \omega_n)}$ such that the corresponding measure admits the following representation with delta functions \begin{equation}\label{eq:MomsAreDiscreteSum} \dif \mu = \sum_n T^n \delta (x - \omega_n) \dif x ~~\Rightarrow~~ m^\gamma = \sum_{n} T^n \omega_n^\gamma,~~ \gamma =0, 1, 2, 3, 4.\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-011: UV mass spectrum from EFT at $t=0$
elements.sent-2411-381: If the integration region $\mathcal{K}$ of the uni-variate problem is restricted to the interval $[0,1]$ (instead of $\mathbb{R}$), we arrive at the Hausdorff moment problem, which, as already mentioned, has a direct physics bearing: it arises directly from the forward limit of the 2-to-2 scattering of a scalar field theory.
elements.sent-2411-382: To be concrete, the EFT coefficients of the forward-limit of $2$-to-$2$ scattering amplitude could be expressed as a sum of moments $\mathfrak{m ^{\gamma_1}$, which are given by \begin{equation}\label{mgamma1Haus} m^{\gamma_1} = \int_{0}^{1} x_1^{\gamma_1} \dif \mu,\end{equation} where $x_1 = {\Lambda^2 /{s^\prime}$ and $\dif \mu = x_1^{N_s - 1 \operatorname{Abs A ( \Lambda^2 / x_1, 0) \dif x_1$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-383: Then, the existence of an atomic measure constructed by delta functions (see \eref{eq:MomsAreDiscreteSum ) means that we can find a UV completion with a number of particles with mass $M_I$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:TIdef3} A(s,0) \sim \sum_I \frac{T^I}{ M_I^2-s} .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-384: In this sense, the solution of the moment problem or the representing measure provides a possible UV completion of the EFT.
elements.sent-2411-385: Thus, this method allows us to re-construct a possible UV mass spectrum and couplings from the IR data.
elements.sent-2411-386: Additionally, every solvable Hausdorff moment sequence $\set{\mathfrak{m ^{\gamma }$ satisfies Carleman's condition \cite{Schmdgen2017TheMP \begin{equation}\sum_{\gamma=1}^{\infty} (m^{2 \gamma})^{-\frac{1}{2\gamma}} = \infty ,\end{equation} which is easy to see by noting the fact that $\mathfrak{m ^{\gamma \geq \mathfrak{m ^{\gamma+1}$, because of \eref{mgamma1Haus and $x_1\in [0,1]$, leading to $\sum_{\gamma=1 ^{\infty (\mathfrak{m ^{2 \gamma )^{-\frac{1 {2\gamma \geq \sum_{\gamma=1 ^{\infty (\mathfrak{m ^{0 )^{-\frac{1 {2\gamma = \infty$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-387: This means that every solvable full Hausdorff moment problem has a unique representing measure.
elements.sent-2411-388: In other words, the forward-limit UV mass spectrum we can construct is unique.
elements.sent-2411-389: The uniqueness of the moment problem away from the forward limit will be discussed in Section \ref{sec:Determinacy .}
elements.sent-2411-390: For a truncated Hausdorff moment problem, however, multiple representing measures may exist.
elements.sent-2411-391: Intuitively, this can be understood because the measure function contains an infinite number of degrees of freedom, so there are many ways to accommodate a finite moment sequence with different representing measures.
elements.sent-2411-392: For the forward-limit, it is sufficient to choose a representing measure with a minimal number of delta functions.
elements.sent-2411-393: As the truncation order increases, as we shall see in the example below, the representing measure will increasingly approach the unique measure from the full moment problem.
elements.sent-2411-394: Let us inject a UV model and show how to recover the UV model from the truncated IR data.
elements.sent-2411-395: For concreteness, we consider a model whose full moment sequence is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:ForwardVeneziano} m^{\gamma} = \sum_{I=1}^\infty \left(\frac{1}{I}\right)^{2} \left(\frac{1}{I}\right)^{\gamma} = \zeta (\gamma+2),\end{equation} where $\zeta(z)$ is the Riemann zeta function.
elements.sent-2411-396: This injected UV model is acutally nothing but the Veneziano amplitude in the tree-level open string scattering with the color and kinematic factors stripped.
elements.sent-2411-397: To see this, note that the Veneziano amplitude with zero Regge intercept is given by \begin{equation}A_{Ven} (s, t) = \dfrac{\Gamma (-s) \Gamma(-t)}{\Gamma(1 - s - t)} = \dfrac{1}{s t} \exp \left( \sum_{k \geq 2} \dfrac{\zeta(k)}{k} \left[ s^k + t^k - (s+t)^k \right] \right).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-477: In general, the determinacy of a moment problem with a more restricted range for the moment variable is guaranteed by determinacy under a more relaxed range.
elements.sent-2411-478: For example, a (uni-variate) Stieltjes moment problem with range $[0,\infty)$ can be thought as a special Hamburger moment problem, which in general has range $(-\infty,\infty)$.
elements.sent-2411-479: Thus, the condition for a solvable Stieltjes moment sequence to be determinate is weaker than the condition for the corresponding Hamburger moment sequence to be determinate.
elements.sent-2411-480: In other words, a Stieltjes determinate moment sequence can be Hamburger indeterminate, while a Hamburger determinate moment sequence must be Stieltjes determinate.

elements.sent-2411-490: A solvable Stieltjes moment sequence $\mathfrak{c ^{\gamma}$ is indeterminate if it has a continuous representing measure $\hat{\rho (x) \dif x$ satisfying $-{\dif \ln \hat{\rho (x) /\dif (\ln x) \rightarrow + \infty \text{ when x \rightarrow + \infty$ and grows faster than $c \gamma^{(2 + \epsilon) \gamma}$ for some positive $c$ and $\epsilon$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-498: In this subsection, we will illustrate the connections between the UV spectra and the EFT coefficients, as well as the reverse engineering from the IR through the moment formulation, using a few specific examples.
elements.sent-2411-499: Let us begin with a simple case where the UV theory only contains a number of scalars with mass $M_I$.
elements.sent-2411-500: That is, we consider a renormalizable theory with a massless scalar field $\phi$ and heavy scalars $\Phi_I$ with mass $M_I$, whose interacting part of the Lagrangian is schematically given by \begin{equation}L_{int} = \dfrac{1}{24} \lambda \phi^4 + \dfrac{1}{6} \eta \Lambda \phi^3 + \dfrac{1}{2}\eta_I \Lambda \phi^2 \Phi_I + \left( \phi \Phi \Phi \right) + \left( \Phi \Phi \Phi \right) ~~~~~+ \left( \phi \phi \phi \Phi \right) + \left( \phi \phi \Phi \Phi \right) + \left( \phi \Phi \Phi \Phi \right) + \left( \Phi \Phi \Phi \Phi \right)\end{equation} where $\Lambda$ is the mass of the lightest $\Phi_I$.
elements.sent-2411-501: From the UV point of view, the tree-level amplitude for the $\phi\phi\to\phi\phi$ scattering is given by \begin{equation}A (s, t) = \lambda + \eta^2 \dfrac{\Lambda^2}{-s} + \sum_I \eta_I^2 \dfrac{\Lambda^2}{M_I^2 - s} + ( s \leftrightarrow t) + ( s \leftrightarrow u).\end{equation}.
